mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses theories of intelligence, IQ, Wechsler scales, and cognitive domains -- the core of intelligence distribution and measurement (D, E).
Examines psychological measurement, individual differences, and the study of human abilities across cognitive and developmental domains (B, D).
Explores reasoning, problem solving, and deduction as intellectual abilities underlying theories of intelligence (D).
Examines the neural basis of intellectual abilities and brain imaging methods used in assessing cognition (D, B.1).
Explores cognitive development and learning in children, relevant to age differences in intelligence and standardized testing (E.4).
Addresses developmental changes during adolescence, relevant to age differences in intelligence measurement (E.4).
Directly addresses the gifted and the upper end of intelligence distribution, including creativity and mental illness links (E.3).
Explores creative abilities beyond standard intelligence, relevant to the gifted and intellectual capacities (E.3).
Examines atypical cognitive profiles and intelligence, relevant to problems in establishing intelligence standards (E.1, E.2).
Explores diverse cognitive profiles in education and the workplace, relevant to the nature of human abilities and intelligence standards (A, E.1).
Traces historical attempts to measure and interpret intelligence by race and heredity, directly relevant to group differences controversies (E.4).
The measurement and interpretation of group differences in intelligence by race remains a contested topic central to E.4.
Heredity and variation shed light on genetic factors in intelligence distribution and personal propensities (E, F).
Statistical methods including experimental design, sampling, and probability are directly relevant to psychological measurement (B).
Standardization, accuracy, and measurement scales are directly relevant to the development of standardized tests and assessment (B.2, B.3).
Explores attitudes -- their nature, functions, development, and how social factors shape them (G).
Emotions function as personal propensities and idiosyncrasies affecting behaviour (F).
Essential for understanding critiques of gender bias in intelligence testing and the interpretation of sex differences in measurement (E.4).
Explores conditioning, intelligence, and learning, relevant to sensorimotor abilities and the nature of human capacities (A, C).
